summary:
The Fall of Ayutthaya (April 7, 1767 AD) was the destruction of the Siamese capital of Ayutthaya by Burmese forces of the Konbaung Dynasty after a 14-month siege — one of the most catastrophic defeats in Thai history, ending the 417-year-old Ayutthaya Kingdom and leaving the city (which had been one of the world's largest, with an estimated population of 1 million, visited by Venetian traders, Dutch merchants, and Japanese samurai adventurers) in ruins. The Burmese-Siamese rivalry had been a defining feature of mainland Southeast Asian history for centuries, with Ayutthaya falling to Burmese forces once before (in 1569) before recovering. The 1767 fall was more catastrophic: the Burmese army under King Hsinbyushin looted and burned the city systematically, taking thousands of prisoners (including members of the royal family), destroying temples, and melting down gold Buddha statues.

The fall was not the end of Siam: the remarkable military leader Taksin, a half-Chinese Siamese general, escaped the siege and within a year had expelled the Burmese garrison and reunified most of Siam under his authority, establishing the short-lived [[event:3247|Thonburi Kingdom]]. When Taksin's behavior became erratic (he claimed to be a Bodhisattva), his generals overthrew him; one of his generals, Chakri, took power in 1782 and founded the [[event:3248|Rattanakosin Kingdom]] (the dynasty that rules Thailand today). Modern Bangkok was founded as Rattanakosin's capital, incorporating the ruins of Ayutthaya's cultural heritage into a rebuilt Siamese state that successfully resisted European colonization through the 19th century.
